coredata.dc:title: QPRTase modified N-doped carbon quantum dots: A fluorescent bioprobe for selective detection of neurotoxin quinolinic acid in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Ranjana
Kashayap, Sunayana
Singh, Vimal
Kayastha, Arvind M.
Mishra, Hirdyesh
Saxena, Preeti Suman
Srivastava, Anchal
Singh, Ranjan K.
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized using microwave assisted green method. It was characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), FTIR, UV–Visible absorption and Photoluminiscence (PL) techniques. The NCQDs were immobilized with an enzyme named quinolinate phoshphoribosyl transferase (QPRTase). The NCQDs immobilized by QPRTase was used as a fluorescent bioprobe for the selective detection of endogenous neurotoxin quinolinic acid (QA) whose elevated level in serum is marker of many neurological disorders such as Alzheimer’s, Huntington’s and HIV associated dementia (HAD) as well as deficiency of vitamin B6. Steady state PL studies were carried out to measure the PL response of the fabricated fluorescent bioprobe as a function of QA concentrations in human serum samples. This probe was found applicable in linear range [3.22–51µM] with the limit of detection ~ 6.51µM. It has desirable sensitivity ~ (0.02340±0.0001) µM−1, excellent stability for ~ 7 weeks and good reproducibility. The similar response of this fluorescent bioprobe for QA detection in triple distilled water and human serum shows that it is unaffected by variation in media. Hence, this fluorescent bioprobe can be employed for QA detection in serum sample for the early detection of many diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQDs
QA
PD
Fluorescent bioprobe
Static Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
TEM, UV–Visible absorption and photoluminiscence analyses of NCQDs
UV–Visible absorption and FTIR studies of NCQDs, QPRTase, NCQDs/QPRTase
Steady state and time domain fluorescence studies of NCQDs and modified NCQDs
Detection of QA in human serum sample
Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of the fluorescent bioprobes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ranjana Singh is grateful to UGC, New Delhi, Govt. of India for providing financial support in terms of BHU research fellowship. Ranjan K. Singh is grateful to AvH Foundation, Germany, DST, India for FIST programme. AS is thankful to DST India (Project Code: DST/TSG/PT2012/68 & DST PURSE SCHEME 5050 ) and CAS-UGC ; Departmental Programme for providing financial assistance. Authors are grateful to Ashish K. Singh, Research Scholar from the Department of Microbiology, IMS, B.H.U., Varanasi, India for providing blood samples. Authors are extremely thankful to Biophysics Laboratory, Department of Physics, B. H. U. Varanasi, India to access the Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer for PL measurement. We are thankful to Prof. Rajiv Prakash, IIT Material Science to provide us HRTEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of NCQDs and inset shows the SAED pattern of NCQDs (b) UV–Visible (black color), excitation (blue color) and emission (red color) spectra of NCQDs dispersed in phosphate buffer solution [0.1M, pH ~ 7.0] (c) 2D matrices of the PL spectra of NCQDs under different excitation wavelength varying from 327 to 380nm. White arrow represents the position of maximum intensity. (d) The international commission on illumination (CIE) diagram for as synthesized NCQDs excited at wavelength 327 and 380nm. The inset show optical images of NCQDs under white light (left) and UV lamp of wavelength 365nm.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of NCQDs (black color), NCQDs/EDC/NHS (red color), NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA (blue color) and NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase excited at 327nm (b) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the modified NCQDs excited at similar wavelength 327nm by blue color and τ0/τ at similar excitation wavelength 376nm shown by red color and time-resolved fluorescence decay profile of fluorescent bioprobe NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase at (c) λex = 369nm, and (d) λex = 376nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescent bioprobe [green color] and after the addition of various concentration of QA (3.22, 6.25,9.10,12.00,14.30, 16.50, 19.01, 21.05, 30.23,37.50, 43.40, 48.30, 51µM) prepared in serum [blue color] (b) The dependence of F0/F on the concentration of QA prepared in serum within the range of [3.22–51µM] (c) Linear dependence of F0/F on the concentration of QA prepared in serum within the range of [3.22–45µM] (d) Comparison of dependence of F0/F on the concentration of QA prepared in both serum and triple distilled water within the range of [3.22–51µM].
Schematic for the preparation of microwave assisted nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs).
Immobilization of QPRTase with EDC/NHS/BSA modified NCQDs to prepare fluorescent bioprobe and detection of QA in real serum sample using steady state fluorescence spectroscopic techniques.
Sensing characteristics of the proposed fluorescent bioprobe NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase summarized along with some results reported in the literature.

row.entry: Radio enzymatic assay for quinolinic acid
Fluorimetric detection of quinolinic acid by catalytic activity of horseradish peroxidase

head.ce:title: QPRTase modified N-doped carbon quantum dots: A fluorescent bioprobe for selective detection of neurotoxin quinolinic acid in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Zoology, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biotechnology, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, MMV, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, MMV, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized using microwave assisted green method. It was characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), FTIR, UV–Visible absorption and Photoluminiscence (PL) techniques. The NCQDs were immobilized with an enzyme named quinolinate phoshphoribosyl transferase (QPRTase). The NCQDs immobilized by QPRTase was used as a fluorescent bioprobe for the selective detection of endogenous neurotoxin quinolinic acid (QA) whose elevated level in serum is marker of many neurological disorders such as Alzheimer’s, Huntington’s and HIV associated dementia (HAD) as well as deficiency of vitamin B6. Steady state PL studies were carried out to measure the PL response of the fabricated fluorescent bioprobe as a function of QA concentrations in human serum samples. This probe was found applicable in linear range [3.22–51µM] with the limit of detection ~ 6.51µM. It has desirable sensitivity ~ (0.02340±0.0001) µM−1, excellent stability for ~ 7 weeks and good reproducibility. The similar response of this fluorescent bioprobe for QA detection in triple distilled water and human serum shows that it is unaffected by variation in media. Hence, this fluorescent bioprobe can be employed for QA detection in serum sample for the early detection of many diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural occurrence of quinolinic acid (QA) has been reported into the mammalian brain (nano-molar) as well as in the human cerebrospinal fluid in several nano-molar to micro-molar amounts (Heyes et al., 1992; Chen et al., 2009; Schwarcz and Pellicciari, 2002). Presence of this molecule (10–40µM) can be detected in pathological conditions (Guillemin, 2012; Leipnitz et al., 2005). Its increased level clearly indicates the deficiency of vitamin B6 in the human being (Brown et al., 1965). Along with this, many diseases might be detected by measuring the elevated level of this molecule (Brown et al., 1965; Guillemin and Brew, 2002; Schwarcz et al., 2010; Guillemin et al., 2004; Birley et al., 1982; Stone and Connick, 1985). Therefore, there is need to fabricate rapid, selective, cost-effective and sensitive sensor with good stability for the detection of trace amount of QA in real biological samples. Many detection techniques such as gas chromatography, mass spectrometry (During et al., 1989; Shoemaker and Elliott, 1991) liquid-chromatography (Patterson and Brown, 1980), thin layer chromatography (Taguchi et al., 1983), radio enzymatic assay (Foster et al., 1986) and capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry (Wang et al., 2013) are available to determine the trace amount of QA in the biological samples. Although, these reported methods are good to detect QA in the biological samples, they suffer from some limitations such as lack of specificity, sensitivity and stability. Some of these techniques are time consuming, costly and involve lengthy processes. Liquid chromatography with fluorimetric detection of QA, whose working principle is based on catalytic activity of horseradish perioxidase (HRP) have been also reported with certain limitations (Odo and Inoguchi, 2009). An electrochemical biosensor Singh et al., 2017a, b) has been reported recently by us for the sensitive and selective detection of QA. In this technique equal surface area of the bio-electrodes are required for reproducibility measurements (Singh et al., 2017a, b). Therefore it is highly required to fabricate better sensor to overcome all these shortcomings. Fluorescent probes have become more powerful sensing devices in recent years because of its quick response, sensitivity, simplicity, reproducibility and selectivity (Zhang and Chen, 2014; Zhang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2015; Liao et al., 2016).

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) exhibiting broader photoluminescence profile are superior to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes in terms of aqueous solubility, less toxicity, biocompatibility, easy to functionalize and good resistance to photo bleaching (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2010). However, the cost-effective synthesis of CDs suffers from some limitation like time consuming fabrication process, low quantum yield and non-uniformity related to their size (Dong et al., 2012). Doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) with other non-metallic elements are beneficial to adjust the structure and composition to get enhanced fluorescence quantum yield. Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are found to have efficient charge delocalization and enhanced fluorescence quantum yield that are useful in bio-imaging and catalysis (Dai et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2015). Highly fluorescent NCQDs have been reported for the sensitive and selective detection of several heavy metal ions (Zhang and Chen, 2014; Zhang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2015, 2010; Liao et al., 2016; Baker and Baker, 2010; Dong et al., 2012; Dai et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2015; Karfa et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2016).

In the present work highly fluorescent and highly stable NCQDs were prepared through microwave assisted green method and was characterized using different techniques like TEM, FTIR, UV–Visible absorption and photoluminescence (steady and time resolved both). The NCQDs were then immobilized with an enzyme QPRTase for the selective detection of QA in serum. The elevated level of QA in serum can be used as a marker for early detection of many diseases. For the activation of COOH groups of NCQDs, EDC/NHS chemistry has been employed (Singh et al., 2017a, b; Srivastava et al., 2013; Sarkar and Nicholson, 1996). The non-specific active sites of the fluorescent probe were blocked using BSA solution (Singh et al., 2017a, b; Srivastava et al., 2013; Sarkar and Nicholson, 1996). Thus prepared fluorescent bioprobe has good sensitivity (0.02340±0.0001) µM−1. It is applicable in linear range [3.22–51µM] with the limit of detection ~ 6.51µM and it is found to be relatively more stable (~ 49 days) in comparison to the electrochemical sensing device (Singh et al., 2017a, b) reported recently. Its applicability has been tested in the human serum samples. This fluorescent bioprobe can be efficiently employed for the early detection of many neurological disorders along with other diseases by measuring elevated level of QA in human serum samples.

Quinolinic acid (QA), 2, 6 pyridine dicarboxylic acid (PD), N-hydrooxysuccinimide (NHS), N-ethyl-N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), bovine serum albumin (BSA), citric acid, urea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and other analytical grade chemicals used in this work were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, India. All these materials were used without further purification.

Quinolinate phosphoribosyl transferase (QPRTase) enzyme was procured from Krishgen Biosystems, New Delhi, India. Blood sample of the healthy individuals have been collected from the Department of Microbiology, Institute of Medical Sciences (IMS), B. H. U. Varanasi, India. The serum was obtained by centrifuging the blood sample at 10, 000rpm for ~ 20min at temperature ~ – 20 °C. It was stored at ~ – 20 °C for further use. Serum (10 µl) was diluted with 50ml phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.0 and different concentration of QA in the serum were prepared and kept at – 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs were synthesized by microwave assisted method. In this process citric acid was used as carbon source and urea as nitrogen doping source. 1mmol citric acid and 3mmol urea were dissolved in 10ml triple distilled water and stirred for ~ 1min to prepare a clear and transparent solution. NaOH was then added to maintain pH of this solution ~ 7.0. The transparent solution thus obtained was placed at the centre of a rotating plate of a domestic microwave oven and heated for ~ 5min at 750 W; within ~ 5min it was converted into yellow color powder. After that, it was dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) [pH ~ 7.0, 0.1M] centrifuged at 10,000rpm for ~ 30min. The final solution was kept at temperature ~ – 4 °C. The schematic followed to prepare NCQDs has been shown in Scheme 1.

QPRTase enzyme was covalently attached to NCQDs. For this purpose COOH groups of NCQDs were activated using EDC (10µl, 1mg/ml) as a coupling agent and NHS (10µl, 1mg/ml) as an activator prior to covalent attachment of the enzyme to NCQDs. The COOH groups of NCQDs were activated by following EDC/NHS chemistry (Srivastava et al., 2013, 2012) and bound to the NH2 groups of QPRTase which results into the formation of covalent amide bond (CO–NH formation) between NCQDs and QPRTase. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 1µl of 0.1mg/ml) has been used to block the non-specific sites of the fluorescent bioprobe. The QPRTase enzyme (10 µl), freshly prepared in Tris-HCl buffer at pH 8.0 of concentration 300µg/ml was added into EDC/NHS/BSA activated NCQDs. Now this fluorescent bioprobe; immobilized with QPRTase was kept at room temperature for ~ 6h. Since QPRTase consists of amino acid residues having NH2 groups hence the formation of covalent immobilization was obtained by interaction between NH2 functional groups of QPRTase and COOH groups of NCQDs (Singh et al., 2017a, b). It was experimentally observed through FTIR spectrum of NCQDs/QPRTase. Schematics of immobilization of QPRTase on highly fluorescing NCQDs treated with EDC/NHS and BSA along with sensing of QA via steady state fluorescence quenching mechanism (static quenching) in real serum sample has been shown in Scheme 2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to determine shape and size of the NCQDs. FTIR (Spectrum 65 FTIR spectrometer, Perkin Elmer) study was carried out to determine presence of the different functional groups and existence of amide bond formation between QPRTase and modified NCQDs. UV–Visible (Lambda 25 UV–Visible spectrometer, Perkin Elmer) and photoluminescence spectroscopic techniques were employed to study the optical properties of synthesized NCQDs and to monitor the changes that took place at different stages of fluorescent probe preparation. The emission and excitation spectra were recorded using an Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a xenon source. The spectral slit width was kept fixed ~ 2nm for both the excitation and the emission monochromator channels during all the steady state fluorescence measurements. Fluorescence decay profiles were obtained using an Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer using the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The excitation sources were light emitting diode (LED) and laser having sharp emission with a low full width at half maximum at wavelengths ~ 369 and 376nm respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, UV–Visible absorption and photoluminiscence analyses of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of synthesized NCQDs have been represented in Fig. 1(a). From TEM images it can be clearly seen that the synthesized NCQDs are nearly spherical in shape. The average sizes of the NCQDs were observed to be ~ 6.5nm. Quantum dots of different sizes have been shown by yellow dotted lines in Fig. 1(a). Furthermore, the diffuse ring shown in the inset of Fig. 1(a) in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern reveals the amorphous nature of NCQDs. The size distribution and TEM images of NCQDs are included in the Supplementary Material [Figs. S1(a), (b) and (c)]. TEM images of probe with 51µM QA has been shown in Fig. S1(d) [Supplementary Material]. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NCQDs has been depicted by black color in Fig. 1(b). Two absorption bands are obtained at 263 and 327nm. The absorption peak at 263nm can be ascribed to π-π* of the C˭C bond while the absorption peak at 327nm may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C˭O bond (Li et al., 2014; Gong et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2014). When excited at 327nm, a strong emission peak is observed at 437nm shown by red color in Fig. 1(b). Corresponding excitation spectrum has been represented by blue color [Fig. 1(b)]. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence properties associated with NCQDs may be obtained due to presence of different particle sizes and distribution of the different surface states [Fig. S2, Supplementary Material] (Liao et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2012). Fig. 1(c) shows two-dimensional (2D) PL intensity map of microwave assisted NCQDs as a function of PL emission wavelength (λem) under different excitation wavelengths (λexe) varying from 327nm to 380nm. White arrow shown in Fig. 1(c) represents the position of maximum intensity of emission spectra. The emission-colors of the PL spectra is blue (0.16, 0.14) at the excitation wavelength 327nm in the international commission on illumination (CIE) map. The emission-colors of the PL spectra is also blue (0.23, 0.27) at the excitation wavelength 380nm in the CIE map shown in Fig. 1(d). The optical images of NCQDs under white light (left) and UV lamp of wavelength 365nm (right) have been shown in the inset of Fig. 1(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Visible absorption and FTIR studies of NCQDs, QPRTase, NCQDs/QPRTase
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Visible absorption spectra of NCQDs, QPRTase and NCQDs/ QPRTase have been shown in Fig. S3(a). The Absorption maximum of QPRTase was obtained at 265nm, whereas; absorption maximum for NCQDs were observed at 263 and 327nm. In NCQDs/QPRTase these two maxima were obtained at 258 and 327nm. The second absorption band (263nm) of NCQDs is blue shifted by 5nm and was obtained at 258nm in NCQDs/QPRTase. Prominent change in the optical density for this band is also noticed. This characterization clearly demonstrates the ground state complex formation between NCQDs and QPRTase. The FTIR analysis was used to investigate the surface functional groups of NCQDs and to detect the presence of amide bond formation between NCQDs and QPRTase. FTIR spectra of NCQDs, QPRTase and NCQDs/QPRTase have been represented in Fig. S3(b) by blue, green and black colors, respectively. The FTIR absorption band observed at 3450cm−1 (Singh et al., 2017a, b) can be assigned as O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [Fig. S3(b)]. The IR bands at 2078 and 1405cm−1 are attributed to stretching and bending vibrations of C˭O (Parvin and Mandal, 2016; Mewada et al., 2014) and C-N bonds present in NCQDs. The C˭C stretching mode is obtained at 1634cm−1. The IR band at 1082cm−1 can be attributed to the C–O bending mode of vibration. It is clear from Fig. S3(b) that intensity of C˭O band has been reduced and the C-N band at 1405cm−1 disappeared in NCQDs/QPRTase complex. This confirms the amide bond formation between NCQDs and QPRTase. These changes have been represented by two colored strips as shown in Fig. S3(b). The UV–Visible absorption spectra of NCQDs, NCQDs/EDC/NHS, NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA, NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase have been depicted in Fig. S4 [Supplementary material].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state and time domain fluorescence studies of NCQDs and modified NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs (~ 38.33µg/ml) and successively modified NCQDs by EDC/NHS, EDC/NHS/BSA and EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase have been recorded by exciting with same wavelength ~ 327nm as depicted in Fig. 2(a). On successive modification, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs decreased gradually [Fig. 2(a)]. Fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F, [where F0 = fluorescence intensity of NCQDs and F = fluorescence intensity of the modified NCQDs] at different stages of modification of NCQDs have been shown in [Fig. 2(b)]. Each measurement was repeated thrice and hence average error was calculated, which is below ~ 2% shown as error bars in the graph for F0/F [Fig. (2b)]. In order to confirm the mechanism responsible for fluorescence intensity reduction at different steps of fluorescent probe preparation, fluorescence decay profile of all these steps were also recorded at two different excitation wavelengths 369 and 376nm respectively as shown in Figs. 2(c) and 2(d). Fluorescence decay profiles at different steps of modification of NCQDs at these two wavelengths have been shown by Figs. S5 and S6 [Supplementary material]. At each step of modification NCQDs were found to contain two different life-times when excited at ~ 369 and 376nm. The parameters associated with NCQDs and modified NCQDs have been represented in Tables S1 and S2 [Supplementary materials]. The life-times of NCQDs were found to be almost similar at the different steps of its modification. At each step, decay life time of NCQDs and modified NCQDs have been obtained and a graph is shown by red color for τ0/τ [where τ0 is decay life time for NCQDs and τ is decay life time for each step of modification of NCQDs, excited at 376nm, Fig. 2(b)]. This parameter is found to be almost constant which clearly demonstrates the presence of static quenching at different stages of fluorescent probe preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of QA in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: These fluorescent bioprobes were employed to detect various concentration of QA prepared in triple distilled water. Detection of QA in triple distilled water and all other parameters associated with this has been shown in Fig. S7 and Table S1 and S2 [Supplementary material]. Applicability of this fluorescent bioprobe has been tested in human serum sample. For this purpose different concentrations, identical to those in triple distilled water of QA were prepared in human serum sample. After that, the fluorescence spectra of the bare bioprobe and on adding QA at various concentrations [3.22–51µM] prepared in serum have been recorded with 327nm excitation. These spectra are shown in Fig. 3(a). It was observed that the intensity of probe decreased on adding QA (prepared in serum) in the similar fashion as observed for QA (prepared in triple distilled water). This shows the applicability of the fluorescent bioprobe for detection of QA in real sample. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) versus concentrations of QA (serum sample) has been shown in Fig. 3(b). The linear nature of the graph indicates towards static quenching. In order to verify it further time domain fluorescence measurement were done with two excitations at 369 and 376nm [Table S1 and S2 Fig. 3(b)]. In both cases two lifetimes were obtained which remain almost unchanged on adding QA. This confirms that the nature of quenching is static. At each step, decay life time of probe and probe with different concentrations [3.22–51µM] have been obtained and a graph is shown by red color for τ0/τ [where τ0 is decay life time for probe and τ is decay life time for each step of addition different amount of QA, excited at 376nm, Fig. 3(b)]. This parameter is found to be almost constant which clearly demonstrates the presence of static quenching at different stages of fluorescent probe preparation.

The linear part of Fig. 3(b) upto 45µM has been fitted and presented in Fig. 3(c). The linear equation giving best fit has intercept (0.943±0.026) and slope (0.02340±0.0001) µM−1. The values of linear correlation coefficient [R2] and standard deviation [SD] for the fitted linear curve are 0.991 and 0.051 [Fig. 3(c)]. The equation of fitted line has been shown by y = a + bx in Fig. 3(c). The limit of detection [LOD] was estimated to be 6.51µM. It has been calculated based on three times the standard deviation [SD] (Singh et al., 2017a, b) rule [LOD = 3 * SD/m] where m is the slope of the linearly fitted graph. The sensitivity was observed to be equal to (0.02340±0.0001) µM−1 which has been determined by the slope of the linearly fitted graph [Fig. 3(c)]. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) versus concentration of QA prepared in triple distilled water as well as in real serum sample has been represented in Fig. 3(d) for comparison. The responses of the probe in serum and triple distilled water are almost similar. Furthermore; fluorescence decay profile have been also obtained at excitation wavelengths 369 and 376nm of the probe after adding QA prepared in human serum sample at all the concentrations. The linear response of F0/F with concentration of QA in serum might be due to presence of static quenching. No prominent changes were obtained in the decay life-time of fluorescent bioprobe with concentration of QA in serum (Table S1 and Table S2). This confirms the existence of static quenching. Details are provided in the Supplementary Material. Several earlier studies on sensing of QA have been compared with the proposed fluorescent bioprobe [NCQDs/EDC/NHS/BSA/QPRTase] in Table 1. It is clear from this table that the fluorescent bioprobe is more stable than the electrochemical biosensor. Similar response of this bioprobe in triple distilled water and in real biological serum sample, detection limit, relatively improved stability shows that this can be used as a fluorescent bioprobe to detect QA, which can be helpful for the early detection of many diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of the fluorescent bioprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Seven fluorescent bioprobes were prepared under similar conditions and their fluorescence spectra were recorded by exciting each probe at excitation wavelength ~ 327nm. The fluorescence intensity of all the seven probes was same with average error ~ 2%. Fluorescence intensity of these probes with error bar has been depicted in Fig. S8(a). In order to check the stability of the bioprobe, its fluorescence spectra have been recorded at an equal interval once in a week upto seven weeks. Intensity was found to decrease by ~ 0.50% at the end of 7th week. The value of average error was found below ~ 2%, which has been shown by error bar in Fig. S8(b). Hence excellent stability has been found to be associated with these fluorescent bioprobes. In order to check the specificity of the proposed biosensor the PL intensity of the probes were measured at same excitation wavelength 327nm for PD (1.8mM) and QA (1.8mM) also keeping all other parameters similar. The fluorescence responses of the probe on adding similar concentration of PD and QA have been depicted in Fig. S8(c). Reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the probes has been clearly shown by bar graph Fig. S8(d). It is remarkable that on adding PD (1.8mM) there is only 15% reduction in PL intensity whereas there is 48% decrease in PL intensity of the probe on adding QA (1.8mM). Each measurement was repeated thrice under similar conditions and the relative standard deviations were found below ~ 2.5%. Error bars have been included accordingly in Fig. S8(d). In-situ fluorescence images of fluorescent probe and on adding 51µM concentration of QA to the fluorescent bioprobe gives clear evidence of the results obtained by fluorescence measurement Fig S9. The probable mechanism of sensing has been drawn from the steady state PL measurements at the different steps of probe preparation and given in Scheme S1 [Supplementary material]. Along with these the UV–Visible absorption spectra of the probe and with different concentration of QA (3.22–51µM) has been included in the Supplementary Material [Fig. S10].

A well characterized NCQDs was modified in many steps and QPRTase enzyme was attached to prepare a selective fluorescent bioprobe for detection of quinolinic acid (QA) in real serum sample and in triple distilled water both. This bioprobe has ~ 49 days stability and ~ 0.0234±0.0001µM−1 sensitivity. The linear applicability range of this bioprobe is ~ 3.2μM-51μM with limit of detection ~ 6.51µM. The equal response of the selective fluorescent bioprobe in water and serum for QA detection might provide a potential platform for the early detection of the neurological disorders and other diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ranjana Singh is grateful to UGC, New Delhi, Govt. of India for providing financial support in terms of BHU research fellowship. Ranjan K. Singh is grateful to AvH Foundation, Germany, DST, India for FIST programme. AS is thankful to DST India (Project Code: DST/TSG/PT2012/68 & DST PURSE SCHEME 5050) and CAS-UGC; Departmental Programme for providing financial assistance. Authors are grateful to Ashish K. Singh, Research Scholar from the Department of Microbiology, IMS, B.H.U., Varanasi, India for providing blood samples. Authors are extremely thankful to Biophysics Laboratory, Department of Physics, B. H. U. Varanasi, India to access the Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer for PL measurement. We are thankful to Prof. Rajiv Prakash, IIT Material Science to provide us HRTEM facility.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.10.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cent. Nerv. Syst. Agents Med. Chem.
